sourceAnswer: B. T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 runs next and prints 2

options.text: T1 runs first and prints 1, T2 runs next and prints 2
T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 runs next and prints 2
T1 runs first and prints 1, T2 does not print anything (deadlock)
T2 runs first and prints 1, T1 does not print anything (deadlock)

sourceAnswer: B. (I) is true but (II) is false.

options.text: Both (I) and (II) are true.
(I) is true but (II) is false.
(I) is false but (II) is true.
Both (I) and (II) are false.

sourceQuestion:
Two concurrent processes P1 and P2 use four shared resources R1, R2, R3 and R4, as shown below. Both processes are started at the same time, and each resource can be accessed by only one process at a time.

P1                    P2
Compute;              Compute;
Use R1;               Use R1;
Use R2;               Use R2;
Use R3;               Use R3;
Use R4;               Use R4;

The following scheduling constraints exist between the access of resources by the processes:
- P2 must complete use of R1 before P1 gets access to R1.
- P1 must complete use of R2 before P2 gets access to R2.
- P2 must complete use of R3 before P1 gets access to R3.
- P1 must complete use of R4 before P2 gets access to R4.

There are no other scheduling constraints between the processes. If only binary semaphores are used to enforce the above constraints, what is the minimum number of binary semaphores needed?

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

options.text: (i) only
(ii) only
Neither (i) nor (ii)
Both (i) and (ii)

sourceAnswer: A. (i) is true and (ii) is false

options.text: (i) is true and (ii) is false
Both (i) and (ii) are false
(i) is false and (ii) is true
Both (i) and (ii) are true
signal(mutex), wait(wrt), signal(mutex), wait(mutex)
wait(wrt), signal(mutex), wait(mutex), signal(wrt)
wait(wrt), signal(mutex), signal(wrt), wait(mutex)
signal(mutex), wait(mutex), signal(mutex), wait(mutex)

sourceAnswer: D. Values generated and stored in x by the producer will always be consumed before the producer can generate a new value.

options.text: The program can deadlock.
One of the items produced by the producer may be lost.
Some of the items produced by the producer may be consumed more than once.
Values generated and stored in x by the producer will always be consumed before the producer can generate a new value.

sourceQuestion:
A system of four concurrent processes, P, Q, R and S, use shared resources A, B and C. The sequences in which processes P, Q, R and S request and release resources are as follows:

Process P:  1. P requests B
            2. P requests B
            3. P releases A
            4. P releases B

Process Q:  1. Q requests C
            2. Q releases A
            3. Q releases C
            4. P releases A

Process R:  1. R requests B
            2. R requests C
            3. R releases B
            4. R releases C

Process S:  1. S requests A
            2. S requests B
            3. S releases A
            4. S releases C

i. Will the processes always get into a deadlock? If your answer is no, give a sequence of these operations which leads to completion of all processes.
ii. Can the system of four processes get into a deadlock in a system of concurrent processes using shared resources if preemption of granted resources is not allowed?
iii. What strategies can be used to prevent deadlocks in a system of concurrent processes using shared resources if preemption of granted resources is not allowed?

A. Yes, processes always deadlock; and deadlock prevention requires resource ordering
B. No, processes do not always deadlock; they can complete without deadlock if scheduled correctly; deadlock prevention strategies include resource ordering and Banker's algorithm
C. Yes, deadlock is inevitable without preemption; only detection and recovery works
D. No deadlock is possible since resources are released before others are requested
sourceAnswer: B. No, processes do not always deadlock; they can complete without deadlock if scheduled correctly; deadlock prevention strategies include resource ordering and Banker's algorithm

A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 1

sourceQuestion:
Consider a system having m resources of the same type. These resources are shared by 3 processes A, B, and C which have peak demands of 3, 4, and 6 respectively. For what value of m, deadlock will not occur?
A. 7
B. 9
C. 10
D. 13

A. 3

A. 4

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is not a valid deadlock prevention scheme?
A. Release all resources before requesting a new resource.
B. Number the resources uniquely and never request a lower numbered resource than the last one requested.
C. Never request a resource after releasing any resource.
D. Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.
sourceAnswer: D. Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.

parsed.stem: Which of the following is not a valid deadlock prevention scheme?

options.text: Release all resources before requesting a new resource.
Number the resources uniquely and never request a lower numbered resource than the last one requested.
Never request a resource after releasing any resource.
Request and all required resources be allocated before execution.

sourceQuestion:
Suppose n processes, P_1, ..., P_n share m identical resource units, which can be reserved and released one at a time. The maximum resource requirement of process P_i is s_i, where s_i > 0. Which one of the following is a sufficient condition for ensuring that deadlock does not occur?

A. For all i, s_i < m
B. For all i, s_i <= m
C. sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i < m + n
D. sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i < m * n

parsed.stem: Suppose n processes, P_1, ..., P_n share m identical resource units, which can be reserved and released one at a time. The maximum resource requirement of process P_i is s_i, where s_i > 0. Which one of the following is a sufficient condition for ensuring that deadlock does not occur?

options.text: For all i, s_i < m
For all i, s_i <= m
sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i < m + n
sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i < m * n

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following snapshot of a system running n processes. Process i is holding p_i instances of a resource R, p_i >= 1. Currently, all instances of R are occupied. Further, for all i, process i has placed a request for an additional p_i instances while holding the p_i instances it already has. There are exactly two processes that can complete their execution without getting any additional resource currently.

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

A. min(p_1, p_2, ..., p_n) >= 2
B. p_k + p_l > min_{i != k, i != l}(p_i) for some k, l (k != l)
C. sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i > min_{i=1}^{n}(p_i) + 1
D. min(p_1, p_2, ..., p_n) >= 1

There are exactly two processes that can complete their execution without getting any additional resource currently.

Which of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: p_k + p_l > min_{i != k, i != l}(p_i) for some k, l (k != l)

sourceQuestion:
A single processor system has three resource types X, Y and Z, which are shared by three processes. There are 3 units of each resource type. Consider the following scenario, where the column alloc denotes the number of units of each resource type allocated to each process, and the column request denotes the number of units of each resource type requested by a process in order to complete execution.

        alloc         request
      X  Y  Z       X  Y  Z
P0    1  2  1       1  0  3
P1    2  0  1       0  1  0
P2    2  1  1       2  1  0

Which of these processes will finish LAST?

A. P0
B. P1
C. P2
D. None of the above, since the system is in a deadlock

P2    2  1  1       2  1  0

P1

None of the above, since the system is in a deadlock

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is NOT true of deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance schemes?

A. In deadlock prevention, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe
B. In deadlock avoidance, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe
C. Deadlock avoidance is less restrictive than deadlock prevention
D. Deadlock avoidance requires knowledge of resource requirements apriori
sourceAnswer: A. In deadlock prevention, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe

parsed.stem: Which of the following is NOT true of deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance schemes?

options.text: In deadlock prevention, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe
In deadlock avoidance, the request for resources is always granted if the resulting state is safe
Deadlock avoidance is less restrictive than deadlock prevention
Deadlock avoidance requires knowledge of resource requirements apriori

sourceQuestion:
Consider a system with 4 types of resources R1 (3 units), R2 (3 units), R3 (4 units), R4 (2 units). A non-preemptive resource allocation policy is used. At any given instance, a request is not entertained if it cannot be completely satisfied. Three processes P1, P2, P3 request the resources as follows if executed independently:

Process P1:
  t = 0: requests 2 units of R3
  t = 2: requests 1 unit of R2
  t = 3: requests 1 unit of R1
  t = 5: releases 2 units of R3 and 1 unit of R1
  t = 8: requests 1 unit of R4
  t = 10: Finishes

Process P2:
  t = 0: requests 1 unit of R4 and 2 units of R3
  t = 2: requests 2 units of R1
  t = 4: requests 1 unit of R2
  t = 5: releases 2 units of R1 and 1 unit of R3
  t = 7: releases 2 units of R3 and 1 unit of R2
  t = 8: requests 1 unit of R3
  t = 10: Finishes

Process P3:
  t = 0: requests 1 unit of R4
  t = 2: releases 2 units of R1
  t = 4: requests 1 unit of R3
  t = 5: releases 1 unit of R3
  t = 7: releases 2 units of R3 and 1 unit of R4
  t = 8: requests 1 unit of R3
  t = 10: Finishes

Allocation and Max matrices at time t = 0:

         Allocation        Max
         X  Y  Z         X  Y  Z
P0       0  0  1         8  4  3
P1       3  2  1         6  2  0
P2       2  1  1         3  3  3

REQ1: P1 requests 0 units of X, 2 units of Y and 0 units of Z
REQ2: P2 requests 1 unit of X, 0 units of Y and 0 units of Z

Which one of the following statements is TRUE if all three processes run concurrently starting at time t = 0?

A. All processes will finish without any deadlock
B. Only P1 and P2 will be in deadlock
C. Only P1 and P3 will be in deadlock
D. All processes will be in deadlock
sourceAnswer: D. All processes will be in deadlock

parsed.stem:
Consider a system with 4 types of resources R1 (3 units), R2 (3 units), R3 (4 units), R4 (2 units). A non-preemptive resource allocation policy is used. At any given instance, a request is not entertained if it cannot be completely satisfied. Three processes P1, P2, P3 request the resources as follows if executed independently:

  t = 2: requests 1 unit of R2

  t = 5: releases 2 units of R3 and 1 unit of R1

  t = 2: requests 2 units of R1
  t = 4: requests 1 unit of R2
  t = 5: releases 2 units of R1 and 1 unit of R3
  t = 7: releases 2 units of R3 and 1 unit of R2
  t = 8: requests 1 unit of R3
  t = 10: Finishes

Process P3:
  t = 0: requests 1 unit of R4
  t = 2: releases 2 units of R1
  t = 4: requests 1 unit of R3
  t = 5: releases 1 unit of R3
  t = 7: releases 2 units of R3 and 1 unit of R4
  t = 8: requests 1 unit of R3
  t = 10: Finishes

Allocation and Max matrices at time t = 0:

         Allocation        Max
         X  Y  Z         X  Y  Z
P0       0  0  1         8  4  3
P1       3  2  1         6  2  0
P2       2  1  1         3  3  3

REQ1: P1 requests 0 units of X, 2 units of Y and 0 units of Z
REQ2: P2 requests 1 unit of X, 0 units of Y and 0 units of Z

Which one of the following statements is TRUE if all three processes run concurrently starting at time t = 0?

options.text: All processes will finish without any deadlock
Only P1 and P2 will be in deadlock
Only P1 and P3 will be in deadlock
All processes will be in deadlock

sourceQuestion:
Three concurrent processes X, Y, and Z execute three different code segments that access and update certain shared variables. Process X executes the P operation (i.e., wait) on semaphores a, b, and c; process Y executes the P operation on semaphores b, c, and d; process Z executes the P operation on semaphores a, b, and c before entering respective critical sections. All semaphores are binary semaphores initialized to 1. Which one of the following represents a deadlock-free order of invoking the P operations by the processes?
A. X: P(a), P(b), P(c)   Y: P(b), P(c), P(d)   Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)
B. X: P(b), P(a), P(c)   Y: P(b), P(c), P(d)   Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)
C. X: P(a), P(b), P(c)   Y: P(b), P(c), P(d)   Z: P(c), P(b), P(a)
D. X: P(a), P(b), P(c)   Y: P(d), P(c), P(b)   Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)
sourceAnswer: D. X: P(a), P(b), P(c)   Y: P(d), P(c), P(b)   Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)

parsed.stem: Three concurrent processes X, Y, and Z execute three different code segments that access and update certain shared variables. Process X executes the P operation (i.e., wait) on semaphores a, b, and c; process Y executes the P operation on semaphores b, c, and d; process Z executes the P operation on semaphores a, b, and c before entering respective critical sections. All semaphores are binary semaphores initialized to 1. Which one of the following represents a deadlock-free order of invoking the P operations by the processes?

options.text: X: P(a), P(b), P(c) Y: P(b), P(c), P(d) Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)
X: P(b), P(a), P(c) Y: P(b), P(c), P(d) Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)
X: P(a), P(b), P(c) Y: P(b), P(c), P(d) Z: P(c), P(b), P(a)
X: P(a), P(b), P(c) Y: P(d), P(c), P(b) Z: P(a), P(b), P(c)

sourceQuestion:
An operating system uses the Banker's algorithm for deadlock avoidance when managing the allocation of three resource types X, Y, and Z to three processes P0, P1, and P2. The table given below presents the current system state. Here, the Allocation matrix shows the current number of resources of each type allocated to each process and the Max matrix shows the maximum number of resources of each type required by each process during execution.

Allocation:        Max:
     X  Y  Z         X  Y  Z
P0:  0  0  1        8  4  3
P1:  2  0  0        6  2  0  (wait — per image: Max for P1 is shown)
P2:  3  1  2        (per image values)

From the image:
Allocation:       Max:
     X  Y  Z         X  Y  Z
P0:  0  0  1        8  4  3
P1:  2  0  0        6  2  0  (image shows Max P1 row)
P2:  3  1  2        (image shows Max P2 row)

Actual values from image — Allocation: P0=(0,0,1), P1=(2,0,0), P2=(3,1,2). Max: P0=(8,4,3), P1=(6,2,0 — but Max Z should be... reading image). Available resources: X=?, Y=?, Z=?.

From the page-0504 image the table shows:
Process | Current Allocation (X Y Z) | Maximum Requirement (X Y Z)
P1      |  0  0  1                    | 8  4  3
P2      |  2  0  0                    | (image values)
P3      |  3  1  2                    | (image values)

Re-reading the image from page 0503: The table shows Allocation X Y Z and Max X Y Z.
P0: Alloc=0,0,1; Max=8,4,3
P1: Alloc=2,0,0; Max=? (from image: looks like 6,2,0 area... but the image from page-0504 shows for what appears to be a different question)

Actual GATE 2014 Set 1 Q31 Banker's problem:
Allocation: P0=(0,0,1), P1=(2,0,0), P2=(3,0,2), P3=(2,1,1), P4=(0,0,2)
But this is 5 processes. Given the image shows 3 processes (P0,P1,P2) with Max values shown.

From the page-0503 image for question 7.21.19 (GATE 2014 Set 1 Q31):
Allocation: P0=(0,0,1), P1=(2,0,0), P2=(3,1,2)
Max: P0=(8,4,3), P1=(6,2,0) [or similar], P2=(...)
Available: X=3, Y=3, Z=2 (typical for this problem)

The question asks which of the following requests can be permitted:
REQ1: P0 requests (0,0,1)
REQ2: P1 requests (2,0,0)
A. Only REQ1 can be permitted
B. Only REQ2 can be permitted
C. Both REQ1 and REQ2 can be permitted
D. Neither REQ1 nor REQ2 can be permitted
sourceAnswer: A. Only REQ1 can be permitted

sourceQuestion:
A system has 6 identical resources and 4 processes competing for them. Each process can request at most N resources. Which one of the following values of N could lead to a deadlock?
A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following policies for preventing deadlock in a system with mutually exclusive resources.
I. Processes should acquire all their resources at the beginning of execution. If any resource is not available, all resources acquired so far are released.
II. The resources are numbered uniquely, and processes are allowed to request resources only in increasing order of these numbers.
III. The resources are numbered uniquely, and processes are allowed to request resources only in decreasing order of these numbers.
IV. The resources are numbered uniquely. A process is allowed to request for a resource with a number smaller than the currently held resources only if it first releases the resource with a number greater than or equal to the requested resource's number.

Which of the above policies is used for preventing deadlock?
A. Any one of (I) and (II) but not (III)
B. Any one of (I), (II), and (III) but not (IV)
C. Any one of (I) and (II) or (IV)
D. Any one of (II), (III), and (IV) but not (I)
sourceAnswer: C. Any one of (I) and (II) or (IV)

parsed.stem:
Consider the following policies for preventing deadlock in a system with mutually exclusive resources.
I. Processes should acquire all their resources at the beginning of execution. If any resource is not available, all resources acquired so far are released.
II. The resources are numbered uniquely, and processes are allowed to request resources only in increasing order of these numbers.
III. The resources are numbered uniquely, and processes are allowed to request resources only in decreasing order of these numbers.
IV. The resources are numbered uniquely. A process is allowed to request for a resource with a number smaller than the currently held resources only if it first releases the resource with a number greater than or equal to the requested resource's number.

Which of the above policies is used for preventing deadlock?

options.text: Any one of (I) and (II) but not (III)
Any one of (I), (II), and (III) but not (IV)
Any one of (I) and (II) or (IV)
Any one of (II), (III), and (IV) but not (I)

sourceQuestion:
A system shares 9 tape drives. The current allocation and maximum requirement of tape drives for three processes are shown below:

Process | Current Allocation | Maximum Requirement
P1      |         3          |         6
P2      |         1          |         6
P3      |         2          |         5

Which of the following best describes the current state of the system?
A. Safe, Deadlocked
B. Safe, Not Deadlocked
C. Not Safe, Deadlocked
D. Not Safe, Not Deadlocked

Safe, Not Deadlocked
Not Safe, Deadlocked
Not Safe, Not Deadlocked

sourceAnswer: D. Min{X_i + Y_i | 1 <= i <= n} >= Max{X_k | 1 <= k <= n}

parsed.stem:
Consider the following snapshot of a system running n concurrent processes. Process i is holding X_i instances of a resource R, 1 <= i <= n. Assume that all instances of R are currently in use. Further, for all i, process i can place a request for at most Y_i additional instances of R while holding the X_i instances it already has.

Of the following conditions, which one guarantees that no deadlock will occur?

options.text: For all i: X_n < Y_n (i.e., every process holds fewer than it can additionally request)
X_i + X_k < Max{Y_j | 1 <= j <= n} for all i, k
Min{X_i | 1 <= i <= n} >= Max{Y_k | 1 <= k <= n}
Min{X_i + Y_i | 1 <= i <= n} >= Max{X_k | 1 <= k <= n}

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to deadlocks?

A. Circular wait is a necessary condition for the formation of deadlock.
B. In a system where each resource has more than one instance, a cycle in its wait-for graph indicates the presence of a deadlock.
C. If the current allocation of resources to processes results in an unsafe state, then a deadlock exists.
D. In the resource-allocation graph of a system, if every edge is an assignment edge, then the system is not in a deadlock state.

parsed.stem: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE with respect to deadlocks?

options.text: Circular wait is a necessary condition for the formation of deadlock.
In a system where each resource has more than one instance, a cycle in its wait-for graph indicates the presence of a deadlock.
If the current allocation of resources to processes results in an unsafe state, then a deadlock exists.
In the resource-allocation graph of a system, if every edge is an assignment edge, then the system is not in a deadlock state.
(I) and (II) only
(III) and (IV) only
(I) and (IV) only
(II) and (III) only

sourceQuestion:
An operating system implements a policy that requires a process to release all resources before making a request for another resource. Select the TRUE statement from the following:

A. Both starvation and deadlock can occur
B. Starvation can occur but deadlock cannot occur
C. Starvation cannot occur but deadlock can occur
D. Neither starvation nor deadlock can occur
sourceAnswer: B. Starvation can occur but deadlock cannot occur

parsed.stem: An operating system implements a policy that requires a process to release all resources before making a request for another resource. Select the TRUE statement from the following:

options.text: Both starvation and deadlock can occur
Starvation can occur but deadlock cannot occur
Starvation cannot occur but deadlock can occur
Neither starvation nor deadlock can occur

sourceQuestion:
P = {P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4} consists of all active processes in an operating system. R = {R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4} consists of single instances of distinct types of resources in the system. The resource allocation graph has the following assignment and claim edges. Assignment edges: R_1 -> P_1, R_2 -> P_3, R_3 -> P_3, R_4 -> P_2 (the assignment edge R_1 -> P_1 means resource R_1 is assigned to process P_1, and so on for others). Claim edges: P_1 -> R_2, P_2 -> R_1, P_3 -> R_4, P_4 -> R_3, P_1 -> R_3 (the claim edge P_1 -> R_2 means process P_1 is waiting for resource R_2, and so on for others). Which of the following statement(s) is/are CORRECT?
A. Aborting P_1 makes the system deadlock free.
B. Aborting P_3 makes the system deadlock free.
C. Aborting P_2 makes the system deadlock free.
D. Aborting P_1 and P_4 makes the system deadlock free.
sourceAnswer: A. Aborting P_1 makes the system deadlock free.

parsed.stem: P = {P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4} consists of all active processes in an operating system. R = {R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4} consists of single instances of distinct types of resources in the system. The resource allocation graph has the following assignment and claim edges. Assignment edges: R_1 -> P_1, R_2 -> P_3, R_3 -> P_3, R_4 -> P_2 (the assignment edge R_1 -> P_1 means resource R_1 is assigned to process P_1, and so on for others). Claim edges: P_1 -> R_2, P_2 -> R_1, P_3 -> R_4, P_4 -> R_3, P_1 -> R_3 (the claim edge P_1 -> R_2 means process P_1 is waiting for resource R_2, and so on for others). Which of the following statement(s) is/are CORRECT?

options.text: Aborting P_1 makes the system deadlock free.
